sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 5 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 168, level 2.
Valid actions right now: LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE, ACTION7.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
Looking at the image, the board hasn't changed much after moving RIGHT. The player is still on the right side of the blue region. The purple region at the bottom is still only partially visible (small strip on left).
The green blocks are at:
- (3, 33) and (9, 33) - top 2 blocks
- (33, 15), (33, 39), (33, 45), (33, 51) - middle row
- (39, 15), (39, 21), (39, 27), (39, 33) - lower middle row
- (45, 15), (45, 21), (45, 27), (45, 33) - bottom row
Total: 14 green blocks
The purple region at the bottom has only 24 pixels at (63, 11), which is a small strip. This is not enough to activate the full purple region.
Wait, looking at the image more carefully, the purple region at the bottom is only visible on the left side. The player is on the right side of the blue region. Maybe the purple region only activates when the player is at a specific position?
Actually, looking at the previous successful attempt, the purple region was fully active (full width) when the player was on the right side. But in this transformed state, the purple region is only partially active even after moving RIGHT.
Let me check if the purple region is active enough to click green blocks. The purple region has 24 pixels at (63, 11), which is a small strip. In the previous attempt, the purple region was much larger when active.
Maybe the issue is that the purple region needs to be fully active (full width) to click green blocks. In this transformed state, the purple region might not be fully activatable because the player is constrained to the blue region on the right side.
Let me try clicking on the green blocks anyway to see if it works. If the purple region is not active enough, it will cause game over.
Actually, wait. Looking at the image, the purple region at the bottom is a small strip on the left side. The player is on the right side. Maybe I need to move LEFT to get the purple region to activate? Or maybe the purple region is tied to the player's position in a different way in this transformed state.
Let me try a different approach: move LEFT to see if the purple region activates, then click green blocks.
Actually, looking at the image more carefully, the purple region at the bottom is visible on the left side. The player is on the right side. If I move LEFT, the player will move towards the left side of the blue region, and the purple region might expand.
Let me try moving LEFT and see if the purple region activates.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Purple region not fully active. Try moving LEFT to activate it.
# Check current player position first
seg = current_frame.segmentation
player = None
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'b' and n['pixels'] < 10:
        b = n['boundary']
        if b:
            player = (sum(p[0] for p in b)//len(b), sum(p[1] for p in b)//len(b))
print(f"Player: {player}")

# Try moving LEFT to activate purple region
action(['LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT', 'LEFT'])
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
